interactions.label: Point Height
interactions.teaches: Adjusting the height of the points shows how their shadows shift on the ground plane, illustrating the relationship between elevation and shadow position.

interactions.label: Light Direction
interactions.teaches: Changing the light direction rotates the shadows around their base, demonstrating how light angle affects shadow orientation and length.

demo_steps.title: Initial Setup: Points and Shadows
demo_steps.narration: Here we have a series of points above a ground plane, each projecting a shadow. Notice that as the elevation changes, the shadow's location and length adjusts accordingly, based on the light source direction.

demo_steps.title: Elevating Points
demo_steps.narration: As we increase the height of the points, observe how their shadows stretch further, moving outward from their base. This illustrates the geometric relationship of elevation to ground shadow position.

demo_steps.title: Rotating Light Source
demo_steps.narration: Now, we'll adjust the light direction. As the light rotates around, the shadows pivot in response. This helps clarify how sunlight angle impacts the perception of an object's reach on the ground.

demo_steps.title: Understanding Shadow Dynamics
demo_steps.narration: Returning the points to a lower height, remember how light source angles and object height interplay to create shadow patterns. This dynamic is critical in both natural settings and computer graphics.

plan.camera_suggestion: Overhead perspective looking down at a slight angle to show both point heights and shadow reach clearly.

plan.contextChunk:
discontinuity, there is an occlusion here (as one surface is hidden
behind another), and the edge shape is only owned by one of the two
surfaces.

In this simple world, if we assume that all the objects rest on the
ground plane, then we can set $Y(x,y)=0$ on the contact edges. Contact
edges can be detected as transitions between the object (above) and
ground (below). In our simple world only horizontal edges can be contact
edges. We will discuss next how to classify edges according to their 3D
orientation.

![For each vertical line (shown in red), scanning from top to bottom,
transitions from ground to figure are occlusion boundaries, and
transitions from figure to ground are contact edges. This heuristic will
fails when an object occludes
another.](figures/simplesystem/ground_figure_points.png){#fig-ground_figure_points
width="50%"}

### Generic View and Nonaccidental Scene Properties

Despite that in the projection of world coordinates to image coordinates
we have lost a great deal of information, there are a number of
properties that will remain invariant and can help us in interpreting
the image. Here is a list of some of those invariant properties:

-   Collinearity: a straight 3D line will project into a straight line
    in the image.

-   Cotermination: if two or more 3D lines terminate at the same point,
    the corresponding projections will also terminate at a common point.

interactions.teaches: Dragging the red line across the scene demonstrates how occlusion and contact points change as the scanner position varies.

demo_steps.title: Initial Configuration
demo_steps.narration: This setup shows the red line scanning through three figures. Notice where it intersects — red indicates occlusion and blue indicates contact with the ground.

demo_steps.title: Identify Occlusion
demo_steps.narration: Move the red line to see how some edges turn red or blue. Red indicates an occluded area where one figure overlaps another from the line's perspective.

demo_steps.title: Identify Contact
demo_steps.narration: Look closely at the blue points. These points highlight where a figure meets the ground, indicating a contact edge.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Orthographic top-down view showing the red line vertically across the figures and the ground plane.

interactions.teaches: As you drag the red line across the scene, observe how the points of occlusion and contact change, illustrating how different parts of the figures interact with the ground and with each other.

demo_steps.title: Intro to Contact and Occlusion
demo_steps.narration: Here we see the red line scanning a scene with multiple geometric figures. Pay attention to the colors: red highlights an occlusion point where a figure overlaps another, while blue marks a contact edge where a figure sits on the ground.

demo_steps.title: Dragging to Discover Occlusions
demo_steps.narration: Move the red line slightly. See how red points appear at different spots? These indicate occlusion boundaries where one figure is partially covering another from the viewpoint of the scanning line.

demo_steps.title: Understanding Contact Edges
demo_steps.narration: Now, notice the blue points as you adjust the line. These are contact points where the object edges touch the ground plane. This interaction between the object and ground is crucial to determine the object’s base contact.

demo_steps.title: Combining Concepts
demo_steps.narration: By moving the red line further, you can see that some areas can transition from being blue, indicating contact, to red, indicating occlusion. This illustrates how figures can obscure each other while still maintaining base contact with the ground.

plan.camera_suggestion: Orthographic top-down view focusing on the figures with the vertical red line intersecting them. Ensure the ground plane is visible at the bottom of all figures.

plan.notes: Ensure color accuracy and distinct labeling for occlusion and contact points to improve understanding as the line is manipulated. Regularly update positions of labels as the line is dragged to ensure clarity.
